violations.rule: Back brevity / atomicity — no 'and' joining two facts
violations.detail: Back contains two distinct facts joined by 'and': (1) distinguishing self from foreign DNA, and (2) initiating strand separation. Should be split into two cards.

If only two repair pathways are discussed and card 11 says NHEJ causes knockout, then card 13 (HDR for precise editing) is trivially deducible. Should merge into multi-cloze or drop one and reframe to test a non-obvious aspect.

violations.rule: Cloze difficulty check — not guessable from context
violations.detail: The cloze 'first {{c1::transcribed}} into a long RNA' is guessable from surrounding context: DNA becoming RNA is by definition transcription. Someone with basic biology knowledge could guess this without knowing CRISPR specifics.

violations.rule: Card type distribution target (~40% why/how, ~30% definitions, ~20% application, ~10% counterintuitive)
violations.detail: The set is heavy on definitional/factual cards (~60%) and light on application/reasoning cards (~6%, only card 16 is arguably application). No counterintuitive/misconception-inoculation cards at all (target ~10%). Missing cards like 'Can CRISPR edit any DNA sequence?' (no — PAM constraint) or 'Why is HDR less efficient than NHEJ in most cell types?'

violations.rule: No counterintuitive facts / misconception inoculation cards
violations.detail: Zero cards in the misconception-inoculation category. CRISPR has rich material here: e.g., 'Does CRISPR work equally well for knockout and knock-in?' (No — NHEJ is far more efficient than HDR), 'Can Cas9 target any arbitrary sequence?' (No — constrained by PAM availability), 'Is CRISPR editing always precise?' (No — off-target effects are a major concern).

violations.rule: Back brevity — parenthetical adds new fact
violations.detail: Back is 'NGG (any nucleotide followed by two guanines)' — the parenthetical explains what NGG means, which is a second fact. Should be a separate card or the card should just test one or the other.
